content.text: Not all examples are equally useful in a few-shot prompt. The choice of which examples to include — and how many — has a measurable impact on output quality, often larger than adding more examples of the wrong kind. Exemplar selection is the systematic practice of choosing examples that maximally reduce the model's uncertainty about the task boundary, label distribution, and expected output format. Poor exemplar selection can even hurt performance: a set of examples that all come from one label class will bias the model toward that class, and examples that use an inconsistent format teach the model to be inconsistent.
Prevents class bias in classification tasks
Using only easy, clear-cut examples of one class
Teaches the model where one label ends and another begins
Only including unambiguous examples — easy cases teach nothing about hard cases
Every example uses the same delimiter pattern
Examples should come from the same domain as production inputs
Using examples from a different domain than the real queries
Recent tokens carry more weight — put the test query last

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def classify(examples: list[tuple[str, str]], query: str) -> str:
    shots = "\n\n".join(f"Text: {t}\nLabel: {l}" for t, l in examples)
    prompt = f"{shots}\n\nText: {query}\nLabel:"
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


# Full example pool — covers all classes and edge cases
POOL = [
    ("This is the best purchase I have made this year!",          "Positive"),
    ("Fast shipping and exactly as described.",                   "Positive"),
    ("Stopped working after one week. Terrible quality.",        "Negative"),
    ("Customer support was rude and unhelpful.",                 "Negative"),
    ("It does the job. Nothing extraordinary.",                  "Neutral"),
    ("Packaging was damaged but the product inside was fine.",   "Neutral"),  # boundary case
    ("Good build quality, but instructions are confusing.",      "Neutral"),  # mixed signals
]

QUERY = "The item looks great but arrived two weeks late."

# Strategy 1: Fixed balanced set — one example per class + boundary case
balanced = [POOL[0], POOL[2], POOL[4], POOL[5]]
print("Balanced:  ", classify(balanced, QUERY))

# Strategy 2: Skewed — only positive examples (bad practice)
skewed = POOL[:2]
print("Skewed:    ", classify(skewed, QUERY))

# Strategy 3: Dynamic — pick the example closest to the query by keyword overlap
def similarity(a: str, b: str) -> float:
    a_words, b_words = set(a.lower().split()), set(b.lower().split())
    return len(a_words & b_words) / max(len(a_words | b_words), 1)

def dynamic_select(pool: list[tuple[str, str]], query: str, n: int = 3) -> list[tuple[str, str]]:
    scored = sorted(pool, key=lambda ex: similarity(ex[0], query), reverse=True)
    # Ensure at least one example per class that appears in top candidates
    selected = scored[:n]
    labels = {l for _, l in selected}
    # Fill missing classes from pool
    for text, label in pool:
        if label not in labels and len(selected) < n + 2:
            selected.append((text, label))
            labels.add(label)
    return selected[:n]

dynamic = dynamic_select(POOL, QUERY)
print("Dynamic:   ", classify(dynamic, QUERY))
print("  (selected:", [l for _, l in dynamic], ")")

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

EXAMPLES = [
    ("Absolutely love it — exceeded every expectation.",         "Positive"),
    ("Complete waste of money. Broke immediately.",              "Negative"),
    ("Average product. Does what it says.",                      "Neutral"),
    ("The design is elegant, but delivery took three weeks.",    "Neutral"),
    ("Fantastic quality, highly recommend to everyone.",         "Positive"),
]

TEST_QUERIES = [
    "Works fine but the packaging could be better.",
    "Worst experience I've had shopping online.",
    "Exactly what I needed for a reasonable price.",
]


def classify_n(n_shots: int, query: str) -> str:
    shots = "\n\n".join(f"Text: {t}\nLabel: {l}" for t, l in EXAMPLES[:n_shots])
    prompt = f"{shots}\n\nText: {query}\nLabel:"
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


print(f"{'Query':<48}", "  0  1  3  5")
for q in TEST_QUERIES:
    results = [classify_n(n, q) for n in [0, 1, 3, 5]]
    labels = "  ".join(r[:3] for r in results)
    print(f"{q[:47]:<48}  {labels}")
# In practice, 3 balanced examples often match 5+.
# More examples help most on borderline inputs.

Exemplar selection is where few-shot prompting pays its biggest dividends. Three carefully chosen examples — one per class, with a boundary-case example included — will consistently outperform five randomly picked examples. The selection criteria in order of importance: label balance first, boundary case coverage second, domain match third, and format consistency throughout. Dynamic selection (choosing examples similar to the query at runtime) further improves performance on diverse input distributions but requires a retrieval step and a maintained example pool.
